subject: Chemistry
solution:

 ΔG°=-RT lnKeq and ΔG°=ΔH°-TΔS°

 ΔH°-TΔS°=-RTlnKeq So, lnKeq=-ΔH°-TΔS°RT=-ΔH°+TΔS°RT

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Calculate each thermodynamic quantity:
A. Reversible isothermal expansion (2 mol, 2→20 dm³, 300 K):
$W = nRT\ln(V_2/V_1) = 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \ln(10) = 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 2.303 = 11,481$ J ≈ 11.5 kJ → II
B. Irreversible isothermal expansion (1 mol, 1→3 m³, 3 kPa constant pressure):
$W = P_{ext}\Delta V = 3000 \times (3-1) = 6000$ J = 6 kJ → III
C. Adiabatic expansion (1 mol, $\Delta T$ = -320 K, $C_V = \frac{3}{2}R$):
$\Delta U = nC_V|\Delta T| = 1 \times \frac{3}{2} \times 8.314 \times 320 = 3,991$ J ≈ 4 kJ → I
D. Enthalpy change (1 mol, $\Delta T$ = 337 K, $C_p = \frac{5}{2}R$):
$\Delta H = nC_p\Delta T = 1 \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times 337 = 7,016$ J ≈ 7 kJ → IV
Matching: A-II, B-III, C-I, D-IV

subject: Chemistry
options:
System A System B
Temperature rises Temperature remains same

System A System B
Temperature remains same Temperature rises

System A System B
Temperature falls Temperature remains same

System A System B
Temperature falls Temperature rises
solution:
For an adiabatic free expansion of an ideal gas, the gas is kept in an insulated container and then permitted to enlarge in a vacuum. Since there is no external pressure for the gas to enlarge against, the work done by or on the system is zero. Since this process does not involve any exchange of heat transfer or work, heat will not escape and temperature of system will rise.

For diathermic container, heat will escape the container and hence temperature of container will remain same.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The molar heat capacity of Helium (a monatomic gas) at constant pressure is $C_p = \dfrac{5}{2}R \approx 20.8 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.

The molar heat capacity of Copper (a solid) follows the Dulong-Petit law, which gives $C_p \approx 3R \approx 24.9 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.

The molar heat capacity of Bromine (a diatomic liquid) is significantly higher due to translational, rotational, and vibrational degrees of freedom along with intermolecular interactions in the liquid state, having $C_p \approx 75.7 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$.

Therefore, the correct order of molar heat capacities is Bromine(l) > Copper(s) > Helium(g).

Answer: Bromine(l) > Copper(s) > Helium(g)

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The given reactions with their enthalpy changes are:
(i) $2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g)$, $\Delta H_1 = -1200$ kJ/mol
(ii) $H_2(g) + Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 2HCl(g)$, $\Delta H_2 = -164$ kJ/mol
(iii) $HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow HCl(aq)$, $\Delta H_3 = -83$ kJ/mol
(iv) $Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq)$, $\Delta H_4 = -663$ kJ/mol

The required reaction for the enthalpy of formation of anhydrous solid $Al_2Cl_6$ is:
$2Al(s) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s)$

This target equation can be obtained by applying the following algebraic operations on the given equations:
Equation (i) $+ 3 \times$ Equation (ii) $+ 6 \times$ Equation (iii) $-$ Equation (iv)

Let us verify by adding the modified equations:
$2Al(s) + 6HCl(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(aq) + 3H_2(g)$
$3H_2(g) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow 6HCl(g)$
$6HCl(g) + aq \rightarrow 6HCl(aq)$
$Al_2Cl_6(aq) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s) + aq$

Summing the above reactions and canceling common terms on both sides, we get:
$2Al(s) + 3Cl_2(g) \rightarrow Al_2Cl_6(s)$

The enthalpy of formation is calculated as:
$\Delta H_f = \Delta H_1 + 3\Delta H_2 + 6\Delta H_3 - \Delta H_4$
$\Delta H_f = -1200 + 3(-164) + 6(-83) - (-663)$
$\Delta H_f = -1200 - 492 - 498 + 663$
$\Delta H_f = -2190 + 663 = -1527$ kJ/mol

Answer: $-1527$ kJ mol$^{-1}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The standard Gibbs free energy change is given by:

$\Delta G^{\theta} = -RT \ln K = -2.3 RT \log K$

First, calculate $\log K$:

$\log K = \log(1.8 \times 10^{-7}) = \log(18 \times 10^{-8})$

$\log K = \log 18 - 8 = \log(2 \times 3^2) - 8$

$\log K = \log 2 + 2 \log 3 - 8$

Substituting the given values:

$\log K = 0.30 + 2(0.48) - 8 = 0.30 + 0.96 - 8 = 1.26 - 8 = -6.74$

Now, calculate $\Delta G^{\theta}$:

$\Delta G^{\theta} = -2.3 \times 8.3 \times 300 \times (-6.74) = 38599.98 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$

Using the relation $\Delta G^{\theta} = \Delta H^{\theta} - T \Delta S^{\theta}$:

$38599.98 = 28400 - 300 \Delta S^{\theta}$

$300 \Delta S^{\theta} = 28400 - 38599.98 = -10199.98$

$\Delta S^{\theta} = -\dfrac{10199.98}{300} \approx -34 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

The magnitude of $\Delta S^{\theta}$ is $34$.

Answer: $34$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
A property whose value doesn't depend on the path taken to reach that specific value is known to as state functions or point functions. State functions are the values which depend on the state of the substance like temperature, pressure or the amount or type of the substance, as a matter of fact, state functions do not depend on how the state was reached or established.

Among the given functions Internal energy, enthalpy, volume,  are the state functions.

The state of a thermodynamic system is described by its  macroscopic properties.

solution:
The bomb calorimeter is an instrument used to measure the heat of reaction at a fixed volume and the measured heat which is called the change of internal energy.
By combustion of 1 mole

C2H6(ΔU)=-Heat capacity×∆Tmass×Molar mass=-20×0.50.3×30=-1000 kJ

C2H6( g)+7/2O2( g)→2CO2( g)+3H2O(l)

Δng=2-(2+7/2)=-(7/2)

ΔH=ΔU+ΔnRT

=-1000-7/2×8.3×300 kJ

=-1000-6.225

=-1006 kJ

So heat released =1006 kJ mol-1

(2) $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$
$\mathrm{R} \ln \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{G}^0}{\mathrm{~T}}$
(on increasing temperature in exothermic reaction $\mathrm{K}_{\text {eq }}$ decreases)
$\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ are almost constant with temperature.

$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}{ }^{\mathrm{o}}= & {[\text { Energy required for breaking of bonds }] } \\ & -[\text { Energy released for forming of bonds }] \\ = & \left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}}+\frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}}-\left(\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}}+\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O}}\right)\right. \\ = & \left(946+\frac{1}{2} \times 498\right)-(418+607)=170 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \text { Resonance energy }=170-82=88 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
A black body is an idealised object that absorbs all the radiation that falls on it and emits radiation over a broad range of frequencies. 

Black bodies emit and absorb energy in the form of electromagnetic radiation.

The frequency distribution of the emitted radiation from a black body depends on its temperature and is given by Planck's law.

At a given temperature, the intensity vs frequency curve for a black body radiation passes through a maximum value. This frequency is known as the peak frequency or the frequency of maximum emission.

At higher temperatures, the peak frequency of the black body radiation shifts towards higher frequencies (shorter wavelengths). This means that the maximum of the intensity vs frequency curve is at a higher frequency at higher temperature compared to that at lower temperature.
